reasoning: The informal statement claims: every group of order 312 has a normal Sylow p-subgroup for some prime p dividing its order. The Lean formalization takes an arbitrary finite group G with `card G = 312` (equivalent to "a group of order 312") and asserts the existence of a natural number p and a Sylow p-subgroup P of G such that p is prime, p divides |G|, and P is normal. In Mathlib, `Sylow p G` represents a maximal p-subgroup of G, and `P.Normal` (via coercion to `Subgroup G`) corresponds to the standard notion of a normal subgroup. The conditions `p.Prime` and `p ∣ card G` correctly encode "for some prime p dividing its order" — without the divisibility constraint the statement would be trivially true (any non-divisor prime gives the trivial Sylow subgroup). The quantifier structure (universal over G of order 312, existential over p and P) matches the informal claim exactly.
